name: 10.25-Inch Cast Iron Skillet
description: The most common entry point into cast iron cooking. Made in South Pittsburg, Tennessee since 1896, this skillet handles everything from cornbread to pan-seared chicken.
cons: Rougher cooking surface than premium options
collector_note: Older Lodge skillets (pre-1990s) with the 3-notch heat ring are sought after for their smoother finish.

name: 12-Inch Cast Iron Skillet
description: Lodge's workhorse 12-inch skillet fits a full meal for a family. Big enough for a whole chicken or a batch of biscuits without crowding.

name: 8-Inch Cast Iron Skillet
description: A compact skillet sized right for two eggs, a single portion of cornbread, or a personal cookie skillet dessert.
cons: Too small for most full meals

name: 10-Inch Cast Iron Skillet
description: Octagonal design with a stainless steel spring handle that stays cool longer than traditional handles. Made in Portland, Oregon with a machined cooking surface.
collector_note: The octagonal shape and spring handle make Finex immediately recognizable. Now owned by Lodge.

name: 12-Inch Cast Iron Skillet
description: The larger Finex skillet brings the same machined surface and signature spring handle to a family-sized format. Excellent pour spouts on all eight sides.
pros: Eight pour spouts for versatile draining

name: No. 10 Cast Iron Skillet
description: Hand-poured in Charleston, South Carolina with a polished interior that rivals vintage Griswold smoothness. The curved handle is comfortable for flipping.
cons: High price for a single skillet
collector_note: Smithey's polished interior deliberately mimics the finish of pre-1960s vintage cast iron.

name: No. 12 Cast Iron Skillet
description: Smithey's large-format skillet with the same polished cooking surface as the No. 10. Deep enough for pan sauces and shallow braises.

name: Joan 12-Inch Skillet
description: One of the lightest 12-inch cast iron skillets made today. Thin-walled construction with a glass-smooth cooking surface, cast in Pennsylvania.
collector_note: Butter Pat is considered the modern benchmark for cast iron quality. Each piece is individually inspected and numbered.

name: No. 8 Cast Iron Skillet
description: Machined smooth like vintage Griswold and Wagner pieces. Lighter than Lodge and designed to develop a better nonstick seasoning over time.
collector_note: Field Company launched via Kickstarter and brought the smooth-finish cast iron renaissance to mainstream awareness.

name: 10.5-Inch Cast Iron Skillet
description: Machined interior with a wide, flat cooking surface and flared sides for easy food release. Made in small batches in the USA.
collector_note: Another Kickstarter success story in the smooth cast iron revival. Earlier production runs are already collectible.

name: 12-Inch Cast Iron Skillet
description: Colombian-made cast iron with a slightly smoother finish than Lodge at a similar price. Victoria has been making cast iron since 1939.
cons: Less brand recognition in the US
collector_note: Victoria is one of the oldest cast iron foundries in South America, operating continuously since 1939.

name: Blacklock 10.25-Inch Skillet
description: Lodge's premium line with thinner walls, a triple-seasoned finish, and lighter weight. Named after the original Blacklock Foundry that became Lodge.
cons: Still rougher than Smithey or Field Company
collector_note: The Blacklock name references Lodge's original 1896 foundry, destroyed by fire in 1910.

name: Signature 5.5-Quart Round Dutch Oven
description: The iconic French-made enameled dutch oven that set the standard. Sand-colored interior resists staining, and the tight-fitting lid locks in moisture for braises and stews.
collector_note: Discontinued and limited-edition colors command significant premiums. Vintage flame orange from the 1960s-70s is particularly sought after.

name: Signature 7.25-Quart Round Dutch Oven
description: The larger Le Creuset round oven handles whole chickens, large batches of chili, and bread boules with room to spare.
best_for: bread baking and large batch cooking
collector_note: Williams Sonoma and other retailer exclusives in unique colors are collectible.

name: Round Cocotte 5.5-Quart
description: French-made with a black matte enamel interior that builds a patina over time. The self-basting lid spikes continuously drip moisture back onto food.
cons: Darker interior makes it harder to monitor fond
Slightly heavier lid than Le Creuset
collector_note: Staub's animal-shaped knob limited editions (rooster, pig, artichoke) are highly collectible.

name: Round Cocotte 4-Quart
description: A more manageable size Staub cocotte that fits a small roast or a loaf of bread. Same self-basting technology as the larger models.

name: 6-Quart Enameled Cast Iron Dutch Oven
description: Lodge's enameled dutch oven delivers solid performance at a fraction of the French brands' price. Made in China but designed in Tennessee.
cons: Enamel not as durable as premium brands

name: 5-Quart Cast Iron Dutch Oven
description: A bare cast iron dutch oven that works on the stovetop or over a campfire. The flanged lid holds coals for outdoor cooking.
cons: No enamel protection against acidic foods

name: Chef's Classic 7-Quart Enameled Cast Iron Dutch Oven
description: A large-capacity enameled dutch oven from a trusted kitchen brand. Wide handles and a well-fitting lid make it practical for everyday use.
pros: Large capacity at a moderate price
cons: Not as refined as Le Creuset or Staub

name: 6.5-Quart Enameled Cast Iron Dutch Oven
description: Regularly rated alongside Le Creuset in blind taste tests by consumer publications. One of the best values in enameled cast iron.
cons: Enamel may chip sooner than premium brands
Limited warranty compared to Le Creuset

name: The Dutchess 6.75-Quart Dutch Oven
description: A DTC brand offering colorful enameled cast iron at a mid-range price. The stainless steel knob is oven-safe to 500F.
cons: Newer brand with less track record

name: Mineral B Element 10.2-Inch Fry Pan
description: French-made carbon steel with a beeswax finish that speeds up initial seasoning. Lighter than cast iron with similar heat retention and searing ability.

name: Mineral B Element 12.5-Inch Fry Pan
description: The larger Mineral B gives a generous cooking surface that rivals a 14-inch cast iron skillet in usable flat area, at half the weight.

name: 11-7/8-Inch Black Steel Round Fry Pan
description: A professional-grade carbon steel pan used in French restaurant kitchens. Thicker gauge steel than de Buyer, with sloped sides built for tossing.
collector_note: Matfer Bourgeat supplies many Michelin-starred kitchens in France.

name: 9-1/2-Inch Black Steel Round Fry Pan
description: A smaller carbon steel pan sized right for omelets and two-egg dishes. Same professional-grade construction as the larger models.
cons: Too small for most main dishes

name: 12-Inch Blue Carbon Steel Frying Pan
description: Made In partners with a French mill to produce carbon steel with a blue finish. The stay-cool handle is a nice touch that most carbon steel pans lack.
cons: Pricier than de Buyer for similar performance

name: 10-Inch Blue Carbon Steel Frying Pan
description: The 10-inch version of Made In's carbon steel line. Light enough for one-handed tossing with the same French-milled steel construction.

name: Black Steel 12-Inch Open Frypan
description: Dutch brand BK makes a well-priced carbon steel pan with a pre-patina finish that shortens the break-in period. Riveted handle stays relatively cool.
cons: Less known brand in the US

name: 14-Inch Hand-Hammered Carbon Steel Pow Wok
description: Hand-hammered in Guangzhou by traditional methods. The round bottom and wooden handle are designed for high-BTU gas burners and wok rings.
cons: Round bottom requires a wok ring
Not compatible with flat-top or induction
collector_note: Each wok has unique hammer marks. The hand-hammered tradition is declining as machine production takes over.

name: 12-Inch Flat Bottom Hand-Hammered Wok
description: A flat-bottom version of the hand-hammered wok that works on any stovetop including induction. Smaller size suits home kitchen burner output.
cons: Flat bottom reduces wok hei potential

name: 14-Inch Preseasoned Hand-Hammered Carbon Steel Wok
description: Comes pre-seasoned and ready to cook, which saves the sometimes frustrating initial seasoning process. Round bottom with a traditional wooden handle.
cons: Round bottom needs a wok ring

name: 14-Inch Pro Chef Flat Bottom Wok
description: A flat-bottom wok with a long birchwood handle and a small helper handle. Joyce Chen helped popularize Chinese cooking in America through her PBS show.
collector_note: Joyce Chen was a pioneer of Chinese-American cuisine. The brand carries cultural significance beyond the cookware itself.

name: 14-Inch Cast Iron Wok
description: A heavy cast iron wok with a flat bottom and loop handles. It holds heat far longer than carbon steel but is too heavy for tossing food.
cons: Slow heat response for stir-fry technique
best_for: deep-frying and braising in a wok shape

name: 10.5-Inch Round Griddle
description: A flat griddle with no sides, built for pancakes, eggs, and smash burgers. The low profile makes it easy to slide a spatula under anything.
pros: Perfect for smash burgers and pancakes
cons: No sides means grease can run off

name: Pro-Grid 20x10.5-Inch Reversible Grill/Griddle
description: Spans two burners with a flat griddle on one side and grill ridges on the other. A breakfast station for the whole family.
cons: Not induction compatible due to size

name: 10.5-Inch Square Cast Iron Grill Pan
description: Deep ridges create grill marks and channel grease away from food. A good option when outdoor grilling is not possible.
cons: Does not replicate true grill flavor

name: Signature 10.25-Inch Square Skillet Grill
description: An enameled grill pan in Le Creuset's full range of colors. The enamel means no seasoning required, and cleanup is easier than bare cast iron.
cons: Enamel can chip on high ridges
collector_note: Discontinued colors in this shape are particularly hard to find.

name: 12-Inch Round Grill Pan
description: Staub's black matte enamel interior works well for searing on grill ridges. The round shape fits standard burners better than square alternatives.

name: Signature 3.5-Quart Braiser
description: A wide, shallow shape that is perfect for dishes that start with a sear and finish with a braise. The domed lid accommodates larger cuts.
cons: Not deep enough for large-volume soups

name: 3.5-Quart Braiser with Glass Lid
description: A shallow braiser with Staub's signature self-basting spikes under the lid. The glass lid option lets you monitor food without lifting.

name: Wedge Pan (Cornbread)
description: Eight wedge-shaped molds that create individual cornbread slices with maximum crust surface area. Preheat the pan with butter for the crispiest results.
collector_note: Lodge's specialty mold pans are popular collectibles. Vintage corn stick and gem pans from Lodge and competitors are widely sought.

name: Cast Iron Cornstick Pan
description: Seven ear-of-corn-shaped molds that turn cornbread batter into golden, crispy corn sticks. A Southern kitchen classic.
collector_note: Vintage corn stick pans from Griswold, Wagner, and early Lodge are among the most traded cast iron collectibles.

name: Mini Cake Pan
description: Six individual mini cake molds in cast iron. The thermal mass produces a crispy exterior on brownies, muffins, and individual cakes.

name: 15x12-Inch Cast Iron Baking Pan
description: A large rectangular cast iron pan sized for pizza, sheet pan dinners, or a full batch of biscuits. Works on the stovetop or in the oven.
cons: Takes up most of an oven rack

name: The Original Baking Steel 16x14-Inch
description: A quarter-inch thick steel plate that conducts heat to pizza dough faster than any stone. Produces charred, blistered crusts in a home oven.
pros: Also works for bread and searing

name: 1.5-Quart Cast Iron Saucepan
description: A small cast iron saucepan with a pour spout. Ideal for melting butter, making small sauces, or heating beans at a campsite.

name: Signature 1.75-Quart Saucepan
description: An enameled saucepan that is safe for acidic sauces like tomato. The cast iron body holds temperature better than stainless options.

name: No. 12 Cast Iron Grill Pan
description: Smithey brings their polished finish to a grill pan format. The ridges are precision-machined, not rough-cast like budget options.
pros: Easier to clean than rough grill pans
cons: Very expensive for a grill pan

name: No. 10 Flat Top Griddle
description: A polished flat griddle from Smithey with their signature smooth surface. Low sides make it easy to flip pancakes and slide off smash burgers.

name: 15-Inch Cast Iron Pizza Pan
description: A wide, shallow cast iron pan designed specifically for pizza. The low rim creates a crispy crust while keeping toppings contained.
cons: Heavy to maneuver in and out of oven

name: 6-Quart Camp Dutch Oven
description: Built for outdoor cooking with three integral legs and a flanged lid that holds hot coals. A campsite essential for stews, cobblers, and biscuits over a fire.
cons: Very heavy to carry on trails
collector_note: Camp dutch ovens have a long tradition in American frontier cooking. Vintage three-legged pots from the 1800s are rare museum pieces.

name: 5-Quart Cast Iron Dutch Oven
description: Finex's octagonal dutch oven with the same spring handle design as their skillets. The machined interior and heavy lid lock in moisture.
cons: Very expensive for bare cast iron
collector_note: The Finex dutch oven's octagonal shape makes it one of the most distinctive modern pieces.

name: Wildlife Series 10.25-Inch Cast Iron Skillet - Duck
description: A standard Lodge skillet with a wildlife scene embossed on the cooking surface bottom. Visible when the pan is empty, disappears under seasoning with use.
collector_note: Lodge's Wildlife Series and other embossed skillets are collected by enthusiasts. Retired designs command higher prices.

name: Mineral B 9.5-Inch Crepe Pan
description: An ultra-low-profile carbon steel pan built specifically for crepes and thin pancakes. The shallow edge makes spreading batter and flipping effortless.

name: Cast Iron Aebleskiver Pan
description: Seven round wells for making Danish aebleskiver pancake balls, takoyaki, or other spherical foods. A fun specialty piece.
collector_note: Cast iron aebleskiver pans cross cultures, used for Danish pancake balls, Japanese takoyaki, and Indian paniyaram.

name: 12-Inch Cast Iron Fry Pan
description: Staub's enameled skillet with the signature black matte interior that builds a patina. The enamel exterior means no seasoning maintenance required.
pros: Matte black interior develops nonstick properties

name: Blacklock 12-Inch Griddle
description: Lodge's premium Blacklock griddle with thinner walls for lighter weight and triple seasoning for a better out-of-the-box cooking surface.

name: No. 8 Cast Iron Dutch Oven
description: Field Company's dutch oven matches their smooth skillet line. The machined interior and tight-fitting lid make it a premium bare cast iron braising vessel.
pros: Lighter than most cast iron dutch ovens
Pairs with No. 8 skillet as a lid
cons: Very expensive for bare cast iron
No enamel protection for acidic foods

name: 12-Inch Deep Skillet with Lid
description: A deeper version of the 12-inch skillet that comes with a cast iron lid. Deep enough for frying chicken with the lid on to control splatter.

name: Carbone Plus 12.6-Inch Fry Pan
description: A thicker, heavier carbon steel option from de Buyer designed for professional use. The extra mass improves heat retention over the lighter Mineral B line.
pros: Thicker steel for better heat retention
